claim: The zodiac and astronomical sections use a systematically different word-order encoding than herbal and recipes sections, reflecting a positional transposition cipher layer applied on top of substitution, detectable as reduced local bigram predictability at section boundaries relative to within-section transitions.
evidence_for: Zodiac entropy (3.7149 bits) and astronomical entropy (3.7471 bits) are measurably lower than recipes (3.8586 bits) and text-only (3.9016 bits), suggesting different encoding density
Belief 0.7 that different sections use different encoding methods
Word counts per folio differ substantially across sections (zodiac: ~108 words/folio, biological: ~332 words/folio)
evidence_against: Lower entropy in zodiac/astronomical could reflect domain-specific vocabulary repetition (star names, month labels) rather than cipher difference
Small folio counts for zodiac (12) and astronomical (8) reduce statistical power
test: Compute per-section word-level bigram entropy H(w_i | w_{i-1}) and compare astronomical+zodiac group against herbal+recipes group using a permutation test on folio-level entropy estimates
test_metric: Mean word bigram entropy per folio, two-sample permutation test p-value between low-entropy sections (zodiac, astronomical) and high-entropy sections (recipes, text-only)
test_threshold: p-value below 0.05 with effect size (Cohen's d) above 0.5 would confirm structurally distinct encoding; p above 0.20 would suggest entropy differences are vocabulary-driven, not cipher-driven

tests_run.test: compute per-section word-level bigram entropy h(w_i | w_{i-1}) and compare astronomical+zodiac group
tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.

reasoning: If transposition is applied only in visually-structured sections (where circular zodiac layouts or astronomical diagrams constrain text placement), this would explain both entropy differences and positional glyph constraints without requiring a completely different cipher per section.
